perturbation.original_text: Payments will be due in US dollars within 30 days after delivery, or when agreed after delivery of installments or the receipt of invoice by Dragon Systems, which ever is later.
perturbation.changed_text: Payments will be due in US dollars within 60 days after delivery, or when agreed after delivery of installments or the receipt of invoice by Dragon Systems, which ever is sooner.
perturbation.explanation: Changing '30 days' to '60 days' and 'later' to 'sooner' impacts the payment terms and could be construed as an attempt to circumvent prompt payment laws, which vary by state. Prior agreements could be superseded by this clause.
perturbation.law_explanation: Massachusetts law requires prompt payment to contractors and subcontractors. While this agreement is not strictly a construction contract, extending the payment terms to 60 days and using 'sooner' can create ambiguity that undermines the principle of prompt payment and could violate the spirit, if not the letter, of the law if such extension is deemed unreasonable. There are other states that have similar prompt payment laws that could be contradicted depending on where the services are performed.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XXI, Chapter 149, Section 29E Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XXI LABOR AND INDUSTRIES Chapter 149 LABOR AND INDUSTRIES Section 29E Construction contracts; reasonable time periods for periodic progress payments and increases in contract price; payment conditioned upon receipt of payment from third party; requirement to continue performance of construction without payment Section 29E Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 29E: Construction contracts; reasonable time periods for periodic progress payments and increases in contract price; payment conditioned upon receipt of payment from third party; requirement to continue performance of construction without payment Section 29E. (a) As used in this section the following words shall have the following meanings, unless the context clearly requires otherwise: ''Contract for construction'', a contract for which a lien may be established under sections 2 or 4 of chapter 254 on a project for which the person whose contract with the project owner has an original contract price of $3,000,000 or more; provided, however, this shall not include projects containing or designed to contain at least 1 but not more than 4 dwelling units. ''Insolvent'', insolvent as defined under federal bankruptcy law or being a debtor in a proceeding commenced under federal bankruptcy law or under the corresponding law of another country, or being a debtor in a receivership proceeding or having made an assignment for the benefit of creditors. (b) A communication required in this section to be in writing may be submitted in electronic form and by electronic means. (c) Every contract for construction shall provide reasonable time periods within which: (i) a person seeking payment under the contract shall submit written applications for periodic progress payments; (ii) the person receiving the application
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the payment terms from 30 to 60 days and changes 'later' to 'sooner', which the Law Explanation argues could undermine the principle of prompt payment required by laws like Massachusetts' (though not strictly a construction contract). Scraped Law Snippet 1, from Massachusetts General Law Chapter 149, Section 29E, concerns 'reasonable time periods for periodic progress payments', suggesting that payment terms should be prompt. While this snippet specifically relates to construction contracts, the Perturbation and Law Explanations suggest that analogous prompt payment principles exist in other contexts. The change from 'later' to 'sooner' creates a direct conflict by prioritizing earlier payment deadlines in all scenarios, potentially undermining the original intent of aligning payment with invoice receipt or installment delivery, and also undermining the 'reasonable time periods' language of the law, potentially leading to non-compliance. The alteration has changed the original agreement such that the payment may not occur within a reasonable time.

perturbation.original_text: MMI warrants providing the Services with due diligence and care in accordance with the specifications set by Dragon Systems.
perturbation.changed_text: MMI warrants providing the Services with some diligence and care as determined at MMI's discretion.
perturbation.explanation: By changing "due diligence and care" to "some diligence and care as determined at MMI's discretion," the standard of care is substantially lowered and made subjective. This conflicts with established legal expectations for service contracts, where a reasonable standard of care is generally implied.
perturbation.law_explanation: The implied warranty of workmanlike quality dictates that services be performed in a skillful and diligent manner. Reducing the standard to 'some diligence' and granting MMI discretion contradicts this implied warranty, potentially leading to disputes over the quality of services rendered. This warranty is generally part of US contract law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: § 2-314. Implied Warranty: Merchantability; Usage of Trade. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-314. Implied Warranty: Merchantability; Usage of Trade. (1) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2-316 ), a warranty that the goods shall be merchantable is implied in a contract for their sale if the seller is a merchant with respect to goods of that kind. Under this section the serving for value of food or drink to be consumed either on the premises or elsewhere is a sale. (2) Goods to be merchantable must be at least such as (a) pass without objection in the trade under the contract description; and (b) in the case of fungible goods , are of fair average quality within the description; and (c) are fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used; and (d) run, within the variations permitted by the agreement , of even kind, quality and quantity within each unit and among all units involved; and (e) are adequately contained, packaged, and labeled as the agreement may require; and (f) conform to the promise or affirmations of fact made on the container or label if any. (3) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2-316 ) other implied warranties may arise from course of dealing or usage of trade. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The changed text, by altering "due diligence and care" to "some diligence and care as determined at MMI's discretion," lowers the standard of care and introduces subjectivity. The Law Explanation specifies that the implied warranty of workmanlike quality mandates services to be performed skillfully and diligently. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 is empty, Scraped Law Snippet 2 refers to implied warranties. The modification allows MMI to provide a lower quality of service than would be expected under an implied warranty of workmanlike quality, creating a contradiction where the contractual obligation now falls short of the legally implied obligation.

perturbation.original_text: MMI shall reimburse Dragon Systems and hold Dragon Systems harmless from any liabilities or obligations imposed upon Dragon Systems resulting directly or indirectly from MMI's or its employees or agents activities under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: MMI shall endeavor to reimburse Dragon Systems and hold Dragon Systems harmless from any liabilities or obligations imposed upon Dragon Systems resulting directly from MMI's or its employees or agents activities under this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "shall reimburse" to "shall endeavor to reimburse" weakens the indemnification clause. This creates ambiguity about MMI's obligation to cover liabilities, potentially conflicting with common law principles of indemnification. By changing "directly or indirectly" to "directly", this clause limits the scope of indemnity.
perturbation.law_explanation: Common law indemnification generally requires a party to protect another from loss or damage. Modifying the language to 'endeavor' creates uncertainty about MMI's actual responsibility to indemnify Dragon Systems, weakening the protection afforded by the clause. Limiting it to directly also impacts the indemnification clause.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: indemnity | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you indemnity Indemnity is a type of insurance that covers a wide range of damages and losses . In the indemnity clause, one party commits to compensate another party for any prospective loss or damage. More common is in insurance contracts, in exchange for premiums paid by the insured to the insurer , the insurer offers to compensate the insured for any potential damages or losses. Depending on the clause of the indemnity agreement , indemnity may be paid in cash or in the form of repairs or replacement. Indemnity also refers to legal exemption from penalties attaching to unconstitutional or illegal actions, typically granted to public officers. [Last reviewed in April of 2022 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE commercial activities insurance accidents & injuries (tort law) tort damages THE LEGAL PROCESS legal practice/ethics business organizations contracts wex definitions business sectors commercial transactions courts and procedure criminal law and procedure legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The change from "shall reimburse" to "shall endeavor to reimburse" weakens the obligation on MMI, creating ambiguity about their responsibility. The change from "directly or indirectly" to "directly" narrows the scope of liabilities covered, potentially leaving Dragon Systems exposed to uncovered losses. This contradicts the principle of indemnification, which aims to protect against loss or damage, as described in Scraped Law Snippet 2: "In the indemnity clause, one party commits to compensate another party for any prospective loss or damage."
